petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented chef from India seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the INA as an alien of extraordinary ability. He had received the 2007-2008 Annual Tourism's Best Chef Award from the Government of India, Ministry of Tourism, and worked as a head chef at an establishment in India. He was previously granted O-1 nonimmigrant status from April 12, 2009 to February 25, 2011, and was approved for an O-1 petition to work for Indian Express. The petition was filed on December 28, 2009.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of receiving a single award: the 2007-2008 Annual Tourism's Best Chef Award from the Government of India, Ministry of Tourism.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director initially determined the petitioner met this criterion. However, the AAO withdrew that determination. While the AAO agreed that the 'Annual Tourism's Best Chef Award' is a lesser nationally recognized award for excellence in the field, the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i) uses the plural 'prizes or awards,' requiring more than one award. The AAO reasoned that not all criteria are worded in the plural (e.g., (iv) and (ix) use singular), so the plural in this criterion has meaning. Citing Maramjaya v. USCIS and Snapnames.com Inc. v. Chertoff, the AAO noted that federal courts have upheld USCIS's ability to interpret significance from singular vs. plural usage. Since the petitioner only demonstrated one award, he failed to meet the plain language of the regulation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted six items: (1) a blurb about dining at an establishment with no author identified and no publication information; (2) a brief article about International Chef's Day and the 6th Chef Awards that mentioned the petitioner only once, indicating he moved from the area; (3) an article about the petitioner relating to his work, but without title, author, or publication information; (4) a blurb/announcement for the petitioner's book, without title, author, or publication information; (5) a snippet announcing the 2007-2008 National Tourism Awards, mentioning the petitioner only once as a finalist, without the author; and (6) the Savvy Cookbook featuring recipes from numerous chefs including a section of the petitioner's recipes, along with a screenshot from www.sirindia.com showing an advertisement for the cookbook.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed this criterion. Published material must be primarily about the petitioner and published in professional or major trade publications or other major media with significant national or international distribution. Item 1 lacked an author and was about dining at a venue, not the petitioner. Item 2 was about an event and only mentioned the petitioner once. Item 3 lacked title, author, and publication information. Item 4 lacked title and author, was merely a book announcement rather than about the petitioner's work, and lacked publication information. Item 5 lacked an author and was an awards announcement, not material about the petitioner. Item 6 was a cookbook featuring recipes from numerous chefs, not about the petitioner relating to his work, and the petitioner failed to demonstrate that Savvy Cookbook is a professional or major trade publication. Citing Negro-Plumpe v. Okin, articles not about the petitioner do not meet the criterion. The AAO also rejected the petitioner's comparable evidence argument under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), finding that the regulatory criteria are readily applicable to chefs and that comparable evidence is not available simply because the petitioner cannot meet a criterion. The petitioner's own acknowledgement that Indian media has focused on food preparers in recent years undermined his argument.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a single letter from a Personnel Manager stating that the petitioner served at one establishment as the executive head of the cooking department, chef de partie, sous-chef, and head chef. No other documentary evidence was submitted.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director initially determined the petitioner met this criterion, but the AAO withdrew that determination. A leading role is evidenced by the role itself, and a critical role is one in which the alien was responsible for the success or standing of the organization. The AAO was not persuaded that every head chef who files an extraordinary ability petition automatically establishes eligibility. The self-serving letter merely indicating job titles was insufficient—the petitioner's job title alone cannot establish that his role is leading or critical. The petitioner failed to submit documentary evidence comparing his roles to other employees at the establishment or differentiating him from others there. Citing Matter of Caron International, USCIS may use letters as advisory opinions but makes final determinations; letters from personal contacts are not presumptive evidence. Additionally, the regulation uses the plural 'organizations or establishments,' requiring leading or critical roles in more than one establishment, and the petitioner only submitted evidence for one establishment.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a letter from a prospective employer stating the petitioner's 'salary will be [amount] per annum with company offered benefits' (future tense); (2) a screenshot from www.tlcdatacenter.com reflecting that the Level 4 Wage (fully competent) for chefs and head cooks in the New York, NY area was $65,291; and (3) a screenshot from www.bls.gov reflecting national wages for chefs and head cooks in the 90th percentile as of May 2008. The petitioner did not submit Forms W-2 or other evidence of actual earnings from Bukhara Restaurant or Indian Express.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed this criterion. A future salary offer does not demonstrate that the petitioner has commanded a high salary—eligibility must be established at the time of filing under 8 C.F.R. §§ 103.2(b)(1), (12) and Matter of Katigbak, citing Matter of Izummi and Matter of Bardouille that USCIS cannot consider facts that come into being only subsequent to the filing. The petitioner failed to submit evidence of actual earnings (e.g., W-2 forms) from his prior employment. The tlcdatacenter.com screenshot only reflected regional (New York area) wages, not national comparison. The BLS screenshot from May 2008 was not the most recent data at the time of filing (December 28, 2009)—the most recent was May 2009. While the offered salary was barely in the 90th percentile, the AAO was not persuaded the salary was high when 10% of others in the field earned more.

outcome_reasoning: The appeal was dismissed. The AAO conducted a de novo review and withdrew the director's favorable findings on the awards and leading/critical role criteria, and affirmed the director's unfavorable findings on the published material and high salary criteria. The petitioner met none of the four criteria discussed, falling short of the three required under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). In the final merits determination under Kazarian, the AAO concluded that the petitioner's evidence in the aggregate did not establish that he is one of the small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor or that he has sustained national or international acclaim. Reliance on a single award, a role at a single establishment, a single reference letter, mere mentions in blurbs and snippets, and a future salary offer without evidence of actual earnings did not constitute the 'extensive documentation' required by the statute. The prior O-1 nonimmigrant approval did not preclude denial of the I-140 because different standards apply and nonimmigrant petitions receive less scrutiny.

fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: On Form I-290B, the petitioner indicated that evidence and a brief would be submitted to the AAO within 30 days. However, approximately 12 months later as of the decision date, the AAO had received nothing further, so the record was considered complete as it stood. The petitioner was self-represented. No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
